exercises.title: Relative Time Words - No に
exercises.intro: Words like today, tomorrow, and last week already point to a time. In beginner Japanese, they usually do not need に before the verb.
exercises.takeaway: Relative time words usually go before the verb without に.

exercises.prompt: Which time word normally does NOT need に?
Say 'I will study today' using a relative time word:
exercises.speakText: きょう べんきょうします。

exercises.title: Specific Time - Use に
exercises.intro: Clock times and named calendar times are specific points on a schedule. Mark them with に when you say when something happens.
exercises.takeaway: Specific scheduled time + に tells when the action happens.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly says 'I get up at seven o'clock'?
options: きょうに おきます。

exercises.prompt: Say 'I go to school at eight o'clock' using に:

slot.hint: The place where students go to study.

exercises.translation: I go to school at eight o'clock.
exercises.speakText: 八じに がっこうへ いきます。

exercises.title: Frequency and Duration
exercises.intro: Frequency words tell how often something happens. Duration phrases tell how long it lasts; they usually sit before the verb without に.
exercises.takeaway: Frequency and duration modify the action; duration normally does not take に.

exercises.prompt: Which phrase means 'for two hours'?
Say 'I study for one hour every day':
exercises.translation: I study for one hour every day.
exercises.speakText: まいにち 一じかん べんきょうします。

exercises.title: Common N5 Counters
exercises.intro: Japanese counts different kinds of things with different counters. Start with the most useful N5 counters and the common irregular readings.
exercises.takeaway: Pick the counter by the thing: people, general items, flat things, long things, or small animals.

exercises.prompt: Which counter phrase means 'two people'?
Say 'There are two people' using the people counter:

slot.hint: The irregular counter phrase for two people.

exercises.speakText: 二人 います。

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses に with a specific clock time?
exercises.title: Specific time needs に

variants.label: CORRECT
variants.en: I get up at seven o'clock.
variants.note: A clock time is a specific scheduled point, so に is natural.

variants.label: COMMON MISTAKE
variants.note: Beginners often drop に, but the schedule point is clearer with に.

variants.label: WRONG
variants.note: を marks an object, not the time of waking up.

exercises.takeaway: Use に for a specific clock time.
exercises.speakText: 七じに おきます。

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a relative time word?
exercises.title: Today usually has no に

variants.label: NATURAL
variants.jp: きょう べんきょうします。
variants.note: きょう is already a relative time word, so no に is needed.

variants.label: COMMON MISTAKE
variants.jp: きょうに べんきょうします。
variants.note: Adding に after everyday relative time words sounds unnatural for N5 basics.

variants.label: DIFFERENT MEANING
variants.jp: 月ようびに べんきょうします。
variants.note: This is grammatical, but it says Monday, not today.

exercises.takeaway: Say きょう, あした, きのう without に in basic time sentences.
exercises.speakText: きょう べんきょうします。

exercises.prompt: Which sentence correctly counts people?
exercises.title: People counter irregulars

variants.label: CORRECT
variants.note: One and two people are irregular: hitori, futari.

variants.label: WRONG
variants.note: 二つ counts general things, not people.

variants.label: COMMON MISTAKE
variants.note: Use います for people and animals, not あります.

exercises.takeaway: Use hitori/futari with います for people.
exercises.speakText: 二人 います。

exercises.prompt: Which phrase correctly counts three minutes?
exercises.title: Minute counter sound changes

variants.label: CORRECT
variants.note: Three minutes is さんぷん, with the p-sound.

variants.label: COMMON MISTAKE
variants.note: Some minute counts use ふん, but three minutes is さんぷん.

variants.label: WRONG
variants.note: 三じ is a clock time, not a duration of three minutes.

exercises.takeaway: 三ぷん is a duration; 三じ is a clock time.
exercises.speakText: 三ぷん まちます。

exercises.title: Time and Counters in Context
exercises.intro: These sentences show relative time, specific time with に, duration, frequency, and common counters in simple N5 contexts.
rows: きょう べんきょうします。
あした がっこうへ いきます。
I will go to school tomorrow.
I get up at seven o'clock.
八じに がっこうへ いきます。
I go to school at eight o'clock.
まいにち 一じかん べんきょうします。
I study for one hour every day.
りんごを 三つ ください。
exercises.takeaway: Use the time pattern first, then choose the right counter for the thing you count.

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the correct English meaning:
exercises.speakText: きょう べんきょうします。

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the correct English meaning:
exercises.speakText: あした がっこうへ いきます。
options: I will go to school tomorrow.

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the correct English meaning:
exercises.speakText: 七じに おきます。
options: I get up at seven o'clock.

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the correct English meaning:
exercises.speakText: 八じに がっこうへ いきます。
options: I go to school at eight o'clock.

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the correct English meaning:
exercises.speakText: まいにち 一じかん べんきょうします。
options: I study for one hour every day.
I study at one o'clock every day.
I do not study every day.

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the correct English meaning:
exercises.speakText: 三ぷん まちます。

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the correct English meaning:
exercises.speakText: 二人 います。

exercises.prompt: Listen and choose the correct English meaning:
exercises.speakText: りんごを 三つ ください。
options: Please give me an apple tomorrow.

exercises.prompt: Match each word or counter to its meaning:

exercises.title: Reading practice - time expressions and counters

cards.jp: きょう べんきょうします。
あした がっこうへ いきます。
cards.en: I will go to school tomorrow.

cards.jp: きのう えいがを みました。

cards.en: I get up at seven o'clock.

cards.jp: 八じに がっこうへ いきます。
cards.en: I go to school at eight o'clock.

cards.jp: まいにち 一じかん べんきょうします。
cards.en: I study for one hour every day.

cards.jp: りんごを 三つ ください。
かみを 一まい ください。
